---
author: "Comfort Adegbenro"
title: "Creating and validating zones"
format: html
---

# Objectives  
I will be joining all the layers, import the joined layers, perform a clustering analysis using k-means in order to create zones, assess how variables are impacting zones and validate zones with yield spatial-temporal stability areas.


# Setup  
```{r setup }
#| message: false
#| warning: false

#install.packages("factoextra")
#install.packages("NbClust")
#install.packages("ggpubr")

# Packages
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(sf) #vector manipulation
library(ggplot2)
library(viridis)
library(ggthemes)
library(patchwork) #combining multiple plots
library(factoextra)
library(NbClust)
library(ggpubr)

```


```{r defining map theme}
mytheme_map <- 
  theme_map()+
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.direction = "vertical",
        panel.background = element_rect(fill="white",
                                        color=NA),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill="white",
                                       color=NA),
        legend.title = element_text(size=10),
        legend.text = element_text(size=9),
        plot.margin = margin(5, 10, 5, 10))

```


# Data import  

Let's import the layers we have produced until this point, including:  
- Spatial-temporal yield stability zones    

- Terrain variables (elevation, slope, aspect, flow direction)  

- Soil ECa (0-30 and 0-90 cm layers)  

```{r sty_v}
sty_v  = read_sf("../data/sy_all.geojson")

sty_v
```

```{r terrain_v}
terrain_v  = read_sf("../data/terrain_v.geojson")

terrain_v
```

```{r eca_v }
eca_v = read_sf("../data/eca_v.geojson")

eca_v
```


```{r soil ph p k}
soil_v = read_sf("../data/soil_v.geojson")

soil_v
```


```{r boundary_w}
boundary_w = read_sf("../data/50ac/boundary/BackFieldPivot.shp") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 6345)

boundary_w
```

# Joining all data together  
```{r all_v}
all_v <- sty_v %>%
  st_join(terrain_v, 
          join = st_equals, 
          left = T) %>%
  st_join(eca_v, 
          join = st_equals, 
          left = T) %>%
  st_join(soil_v, 
          join = st_equals, 
          left = T) %>%
  relocate(geometry, .after = last_col())

all_v
```

# Exporting  
```{r}
write_sf(all_v, 
         "../data/all_v.geojson",
         delete_dsn = T)
```

```{r all_v}
all_v = read_sf("../data/all_v.geojson")

all_v
```

```{r boundary_w}
boundary_w = read_sf("../data/50ac/boundary/BackFieldPivot.shp") %>%
  st_transform(crs = 6345)

boundary_w
```


# EDA  
```{r summary}
summary(all_v)
```


# k-means in theory  
k-means is a unsupervised clustering algorithm that partitions the data into k groups, where k is defined by the user (i.e., you).  

If you have a y-variable that is the predicted variable, and you provide the y variable, it is supervised analysis. But analysis that are unsupervised do not take y variables, but only x variables. You are not giving the labels, and the analysis is supposed to find the commonality.

## How does k-mean works?  
Let's look at the example below:  
```{r k-means pre-clustering}
knitr::include_graphics("https://agronomy.netlify.app/img/kmeans0.png")
```
> How many groups do we have above?  
Can start by specifying a number of clusters (k) that makes sense. 

I can't see the figure, but from the class, it is 3 groups.

However, sometimes it is **not that easy to visually assess** it if have many variables (each variable adds a dimension, how do you visualize a data set with 10 variables that we want to use for clustering?)

Coming back to this example, here it is clear that k=3, so the **first step of the algorithm is to randomly select** 3 observations in the data set and have them as the cluster centers:  
```{r k-means step1}
knitr::include_graphics("https://agronomy.netlify.app/img/kmeans1.png")
```
- Once the cluster centers have been created, the algorithm calculates the **distance of all observations to each of the clusters centers**, and 

- each observation of the entire data set is **assigned a cluster membership** to the cluster center (mean) closest to that observation (check this on the plot above).  

- At this point, the algorithm uses all members of a cluster and **recalculates the cluster mean** (not an actual observation in the data as it was on the first step)  

- The algorithm repeats the entire process until **cluster means stabilize**
This is when the algorithm converges and the center does not change and this center does not have to be an observation.

Let's check the entire process for this example below:
```{r k-means in action}
knitr::include_graphics("https://miro.medium.com/max/960/1*KrcZK0xYgTa4qFrVr0fO2w.gif")
```

## k-means properties  
k-means:  
- Is used for clustering (e.g., management zone creation)  
- Is an **unsupervised** analysis (no outcome/response/y on a `y ~ x` formula)    
- Only takes **predictors** (i.e., explanatory variables/x on a `y ~ x` formula).    
- Predictors need to be **numerical**  (good bye flowdir)  

## k-means shortcomings  
k-means is useful when clusters are circular (not in a spatial context, but in a x vs. y plot context), but can fail badly when clusters have **odd shapes or outliers**.  

Let's check how k-means (first column below) compares to other clustering algorithms (remaining columns) in classifying data sets with different shapes (rows):  
```{r clustering algorithms comparison}
knitr::include_graphics("https://miro.medium.com/max/1400/1*oNt9G9UpVhtyFLDBwEMf8Q.png")

```
> So, can we always use k-means for clustering?  

However, it can be **difficult to visually assess clustering performance on data sets with many predictors** (remember, each variable adds one dimension, our brain can make sense of 4-5 dimensions max).  

**The curse of dimensionality!**

We can make use of some machine learning techniques like data train/validation/test splits and select model that most accurately predicts new data (beyond the scope of our class).  

# k-means in practice  
## Data prep for k-means  
Before running k-means, we need to make sure that our data:  
- **does not contain NA values** (k-means doesn't handle NAs and just throughs an error). Even a single cell with NA will cause issues. 

- only contains **numerical columns** (k-means doesn't handle categorical variables)  

- is **standardized**, so variables with large magnitude don't disproportianlly imapct the results over variables with smaller magnitude (e.g., slope 0-7% while aspect 0-360 degrees, see summary below).

```{r}
summary(all_v)
```


```{r @ all_v_n}
all_v_n = all_v %>%
  # Dropping NA cells
  drop_na() %>%
  # Selecting variables of interest
  dplyr::select(elev_m:ph, k_lbac, p_lbac,
                -flowdir) %>%
  # Standardizing 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~scale(.x)))
all_v_n

summary(all_v_n)
```

## Defining k  
We need to define the number of clusters we want.  
Let's try 4.  

```{r kmeans initial model }
mod_km = kmeans(all_v_n %>%
                  st_drop_geometry(),
                centers = 10,
                nstart = 10)

mod_km
```
The argument `nstart` defines how many times the algorithm should be run. This is important because of the random nature of selecting the observations on the first step. Having nstart=10 runs the model 10 times and avoids an unfortunate initial random selection that ends up creating clusters that do not represent the true data groups.  

With **k=4**, our between/total SS was 56.9% (greater the better).    
Let's try **k=3** and see what happens: between/total SS was 49.3%.  

What about **k=10**? between/total SS was 72.9%.

> So let's just select k=10, right?  

The thing is that increasing k will always increase between/total SS.  We need to find the **sweet spot** where we have **enough ks that represent the actual groups within our data**, but no more that that.

Also, think in a **PA application**. If your field really is highly variable and requires a large number of zones (i.e., k=10), then it is what it is. But if your field only truly has 2-3 zones, creating 10 zones adds extra complexity for the grower without really bringing the benefits (because 2-3 zones would've sufficed).

So how do we **find the best k** value for a given data set?    

## Finding k  
Since the choice of k can be subjective, we will need to find an **objective** way to select the value of k that most properly represents our data set.  

There are different tests and metrics that can be used to select k.  
All of them run k-means with k ranging from 1 to 10 (in our case), and assess how much information is gained by adding each of the extra groups beyond 1.  

Let's explore a few of these metrics:
```{r finding k - wss method}
# Total error x k
fviz_nbclust(all_v_n %>%
               st_drop_geometry(),
             method = "wss",
             k.max = 10,
             FUNcluster = kmeans)

```
This is called an elbow plot. We use the corner of the elbow to determine the k value.

```{r finding k - silhouette method}
# Silhouette width
fviz_nbclust(all_v_n %>%
               st_drop_geometry(), 
             method = "s",
             k.max = 10,
             FUNcluster = kmeans) #2

```
What if different metrics give you a different recommendation on k?  

We can compute multiple metrics, and select the k value recommended by the majority:

**NOTE**: the code below took 5 minutes to run on my computer. **DO NOT RUN IT IN CLASS!**. You can run it later to check the result if you wish.  
```{r finding k - multi-metric vote}
# Voting from 26 indices  
bestk <- NbClust(all_v_n %>%
                   st_drop_geometry(),
                 distance = "euclidean", 
                 method ="kmeans", 
                 index= "all",
                 min.nc = 2, 
                 max.nc = 6)

fviz_nbclust(bestk) # 13 tests proposed 2 as best

```

I ran the analysis and it shows 2 clusters as the best.
Let's go with 2 clusters:
```{r @ mod_km2 }
mod_km2 = kmeans(all_v_n %>%
                   st_drop_geometry(),
                 centers = 2,
                 nstart = 10)

mod_km2
```

# Exploring clusters  
Let's save cluster membership as a column in our data, and bring back the geometry so we can map it.  
```{r @ zone_df }
zone_df = all_v_n %>%
  # Adding cluster membership column
  mutate(cluster = mod_km2$cluster) %>%
  mutate(cluster = factor(cluster))

zone_df %>%
  group_by(cluster)%>%
  tally()

```


```{r cluster map}
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = zone_df,
          aes(fill = cluster),
          color = NA) +
  scale_fill_colorblind() +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill = NA,
          linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Unsmoothed zones") +
  theme_map() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color = "blue")) +
  mytheme_map +
  annotation_scale(location = "bl",
                   width_hint = 0.3,
                   pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                   pad_y = unit(0.01, "in")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "tl",
                       pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                       pad_y = unit(0.05, "in"),
                       height = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       width = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering())

ggsave("../output/zones_unsmoothed.png",
       width = 6,
       height = 4)
```

# Smoothing zones  
```{r what is a focal window?}
knitr::include_graphics("https://r.geocompx.org/images/04_focal_example.png")

```

```{r grid_r}
library(stars)
# grid in vector format
grid_r <-  boundary_w %>%
  st_make_grid(cellsize = 5) %>%
  st_as_sf() %>%
  st_rasterize(dx=5, dy=5) %>%
  st_crop(boundary_w)
```

## 3 x 3

```{r smoothing as 3 x 3 mean raster}
library(starsExtra)
library(gstat)

zone_s_3m = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,3,3),
         fun = "mean") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_3m


```

```{r 3x3 mean smoothed plot}
mean_map3 = zone_s_3m %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="3x3, Mean")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map +
  annotation_scale(location = "bl",
                   width_hint = 0.3,
                   pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                   pad_y = unit(0.01, "in")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "tl",
                       pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                       pad_y = unit(0.05, "in"),
                       height = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       width = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering())
  

mean_map3


```


```{r smoothing as 3 x 3 min raster}


zone_s_3min = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,3,3),
         fun = "min") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_3min


```

```{r 3x3 min smoothed plot}
min_map3 = zone_s_3min %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="3x3, Min")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

min_map3
```


```{r smoothing as 3 x 3 max raster}


zone_s_3max = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,3,3),
         fun = "max") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_3max


```

```{r 3x3 max smoothed plot}
max_map3 = zone_s_3max %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="3x3, Max")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

max_map3
```

```{r combining 3 x 3 maps}
mean_map3 + min_map3 + max_map3 + 
  plot_layout(ncol = 3) +
  plot_annotation(title = "3 x 3 Smoothed Maps",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)))

ggsave("../output/3x3_maps.png",
       width = 15,
       height = 5)
```


## 5 x 5


```{r smoothing as 5 x 5 mean raster}
library(starsExtra)
library(gstat)

zone_s_5m = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,5,5),
         fun = "mean") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_5m


```

```{r 5x5 mean smoothed plot}
mean_map5 = zone_s_5m %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="5x5, Mean")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map +
  annotation_scale(location = "bl",
                   width_hint = 0.3,
                   pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                   pad_y = unit(0.01, "in")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "tl",
                       pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                       pad_y = unit(0.05, "in"),
                       height = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       width = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering())
  

mean_map5


```


```{r smoothing as  5x 5 min raster}


zone_s_5min = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,5,5),
         fun = "min") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_5min


```

```{r 5x5 min smoothed plot}
min_map5 = zone_s_5min %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="5x5, Min")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

min_map5
```


```{r smoothing as 5 x 5 max raster}


zone_s_5max = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,5,5),
         fun = "max") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_5max


```

```{r 5x5 max smoothed plot}
max_map5 = zone_s_5max %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="5x5, Max")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

max_map5
```

```{r combining 5x5 maps}
mean_map5 + min_map5 + max_map5 +
  plot_layout(ncol = 3) +
  plot_annotation(title = "5 x 5 Smoothed Maps",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)))

ggsave("../output/5x5_maps.png",
       width = 15,
       height = 5)

```


## 7 x 7


```{r smoothing as 7 x 7 mean raster}

zone_s_7m = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,7,7),
         fun = "mean") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_7m


```

```{r 7x7 mean smoothed plot}
mean_map7 = zone_s_7m %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="7x7, Mean")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map +
  annotation_scale(location = "bl",
                   width_hint = 0.3,
                   pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                   pad_y = unit(0.01, "in")) +
  annotation_north_arrow(location = "tl",
                       pad_x = unit(0.1, "in"),
                       pad_y = unit(0.05, "in"),
                       height = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       width = unit(0.4, "in"),
                       style = north_arrow_fancy_orienteering())
  

mean_map7


```


```{r smoothing as 7 x 7 min raster}


zone_s_7min = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,7,7),
         fun = "min") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_7min


```

```{r 7x7 min smoothed plot}
min_map7 = zone_s_7min %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="7x7, Min")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

min_map7
```


```{r smoothing as 7 x 7 max raster}


zone_s_7max = zone_df %>%
  dplyr::select(cluster) %>%
  # Transforming from polygon to point
  st_cast("POINT") %>%
  # Transforming from geometry to xy (needed by the focal function)
  st_sfc2xy() %>%
  # Transforming from point (vector) to raster
  st_as_stars() %>%
  # Applying focal filter
  focal2(w = matrix(1,7,7),
         fun = "max") %>%
  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%
  # Interpolating to fill to boundary
  gstat(formula = cluster ~ 1,
        data = .) %>%
  predict(grid_r) %>%

  # Transforming from raster back to vector
  st_as_sf() %>%

  # Rounding  
  mutate(cluster_f = round(var1.pred, 0)) %>%
  # Adjusting cluster id from numeric to factor
  mutate(cluster_f = factor(cluster_f)) %>%
  st_intersection(boundary_w)

zone_s_7max


```

```{r 7x7 max smoothed plot}
max_map7 = zone_s_7max %>%
  ggplot()+
  geom_sf(aes(fill = cluster_f),
          color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = boundary_w, fill=NA,
          linewidth = 1)+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  labs(title="7x7, Max")+
  theme_map()+
  theme(plot.title = element_text(color="blue")) +
  mytheme_map

max_map7
```


```{r combining 7x7 maps}
mean_map7 + min_map7 + max_map7 +
  plot_layout(ncol = 3) +
  plot_annotation(title = "7 x 7 Smoothed Maps",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)))

ggsave("../output/7x7_maps.png",
       width = 15,
       height = 5)

```

## Final smoothed map cluster
```{r map of final smoothed cluster/zone}
mean_map7 +
  plot_annotation(title = "Final Smoothed Zone Map: 7 x 7 Mean",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)))

ggsave("../output/final7x7_mean_map.png",
       width = 8,
       height = 5)
  


```



How are clusters affected by the variables used to create them?  

```{r}
zone_s_df = zone_s_7m %>%
  dplyr::select(-var1.var) %>%
  st_join(all_v,
          join = st_nearest_feature,
          left = T) %>%
  dplyr::select(-flowdir, -aspect)

zone_s_df

zone_s_df %>%
  summary

nrow(zone_s_df) == nrow(zone_s_7m)

summary(zone_s_7m)
```



```{r cluster x variable boxplots}
zone_s_df %>%
  dplyr::select(-stclass, -ph_class, -klbac_class, -plbac_class) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = elev_m:k_lbac) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=cluster_f, y=value, color=cluster_f))+
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = F)+
  scale_color_colorblind()+
  facet_wrap(~name, scales="free_y", ncol=3)+
  stat_compare_means(label = "p.format",
                     hjust = -.1,
                     vjust=1)+
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

> Based on the plots above and what we established about high- and low-stable classes in the previous exercise, which cluster do you expect that will be higher yielding? Why?    





# Validating clusters  
Ok, so we have 2 clusters that are significantly different based on the variables we used to create them. Great!  

> What does that mean for yield though? 
> Are these two clusters creating different yield levels?  
> How can we test that?  



```{r clusters and standardized yield}
zone_s_df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = sy16c:sy24c) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=cluster_f, y=value, color=cluster_f))+
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = F)+
  scale_color_colorblind()+
  facet_wrap(~name, scales="free_y", ncol=3)+
  stat_compare_means(label = "p.format",
                     hjust = -.1,
                     vjust=1)+
  theme(legend.position = "none")

```

```{r clusters and mean yield and cv }
zone_s_df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(mean_pixel, cv_pixel)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=cluster_f, y=value, color=cluster_f))+
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = F)+
  scale_color_colorblind()+
  facet_wrap(~name, scales="free_y", ncol=3)+
  stat_compare_means(label = "p.format",
                     hjust = -.1,
                     vjust=1)+
  theme(legend.position = "none")

```

It seems like cluster 1 has higher CV and lower yield, and cluster 2 has lower CV and greater yield.  

```{r contingency table}
zone_s_df %>%
  group_by(stclass) %>%
  mutate(N=length(stclass)) %>%
  group_by(cluster_f, stclass, N) %>%
  tally() %>%
  mutate(prop=(n/N)*100) %>%
  mutate(prop=round(prop,0)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x=cluster_f, 
             y=prop, 
             fill=cluster_f))+
  geom_col(position="dodge", color="black")+
  scale_fill_colorblind()+
  facet_grid(~stclass )+
  geom_text(aes(label=paste0(prop,"%"), y=prop+5))+
  theme(legend.position = "none")

zone_s_df %>%
  count(stclass)

summary(zone_s_df)

```

I suppose we can call cluster 2 as high yield and cluster 1 as lower yield, right?  

# Exporting clusters  
```{r exporting clusters}
zone_s_df %>%
  mutate(zone = ifelse(cluster_f == "2",
                      "high",
                      "low")) %>%
  write_sf("../data/zone_s.geojson",
           delete_dsn = T)
```
